learn.openingTension: A monopolist faces a downward-sloping demand curve and can set any price — but higher prices mean fewer units sold. The key question is: does cutting price raise or lower total revenue? The answer is entirely determined by elasticity, and the marginal revenue formula MR = P(1 + 1/ε) encodes this link in a single equation that drives all monopoly pricing analysis.
learn.coreIdea: Total revenue TR = P·Q and marginal revenue MR = dTR/dQ = P(1 + 1/ε) capture how revenue responds to output changes. Because ε < 0 on a downward-sloping demand curve, MR < P always; the sign of MR depends on whether |ε| exceeds or falls short of 1. A profit-maximizing monopolist always operates where |ε| > 1 because MR < 0 in the inelastic region means producing more units destroys revenue.
learn.mathSetup: TR = P(Q)·Q. MR = dTR/dQ = P + Q(dP/dQ) = P[1 + (Q/P)(dP/dQ)] = P[1 + 1/ε]. Since ε = (dQ/dP)(P/Q), we have 1/ε = (dP/dQ)(Q/P). Key results: MR > 0 ⟺ |ε| > 1; MR = 0 ⟺ |ε| = 1 (TR maximum); MR < 0 ⟺ |ε| < 1. For linear inverse demand P = a − bQ: MR = a − 2bQ (same intercept, twice the slope).
learn.derivation: 1. Write TR = P·Q where P = P(Q) via inverse demand. Economic reason: a firm with market power faces the entire demand curve and chooses Q (or equivalently P). 2. Differentiate: MR = dTR/dQ = P + Q(dP/dQ). Economic reason: selling one more unit yields P on the new unit, but requires lowering price on all Q existing units by dP/dQ per unit. 3. Factor out P: MR = P[1 + (Q/P)(dP/dQ)]. Economic reason: rearranges to involve the inverse of elasticity. 4. Recognize (Q/P)(dP/dQ) = 1/ε (since ε = (dQ/dP)(P/Q)). Economic reason: this substitution converts the slope into the elasticity, making the formula unit-free and general. 5. Result: MR = P(1 + 1/ε). Since ε < 0, 1/ε < 0, so MR < P. Economic reason: the firm must cut price to sell more, making each unit's revenue less than the price. 6. For TR maximization, set MR = 0: P(1 + 1/ε) = 0 → ε = −1 (unit elastic). Economic reason: at unit elasticity, the revenue gain from one more unit exactly offsets the revenue loss from lower price across all existing units.
learn.graphicalIntuition: On a price-quantity diagram, draw the linear inverse demand P = a − bQ and directly below it the MR curve P = a − 2bQ (same vertical intercept, twice the slope). MR hits the Q-axis at exactly half the demand curve's Q-intercept. Total revenue is the rectangle P·Q; it rises to the left of the MR=0 point (elastic region) and falls to the right (inelastic region). On a graph, the TR curve (on a separate panel) is an inverted U-shape, peaking at the quantity where MR=0 and |ε|=1. A monopolist sets MR=MC, which must intersect MR in its positive portion — confirming the monopolist's output is always in the elastic region of demand. Common Misreading: students draw the MR curve with the same slope as demand; the correct MR for linear inverse demand has twice the slope, reaching zero at half the demand's quantity intercept.
learn.workedExample: Given: Inverse demand P = 40 − 2Q. Find: MR, TR-maximizing Q, monopolist's quantity if MC = 8. Solution: MR = 40 − 4Q (same intercept 40, twice slope). TR maximum: MR = 0 → 40 − 4Q = 0 → Q = 10, P = 40 − 20 = $20, TR = $200. Monopolist: MR = MC → 40 − 4Q = 8 → Q = 8, P = 40 − 16 = $24. Check elasticity at Q=8: dQ/dP from Q = 20 − 0.5P gives dQ/dP = −0.5; ε = −0.5(24/8) = −1.5. |ε| = 1.5 > 1 ✓ (elastic region, consistent with MR = 8 > 0). Interpret: The monopolist produces 8 units at $24, which is in the elastic region. If MC were zero, the optimal Q would be 10 (TR maximum), confirming that positive MC pulls Q below the TR-maximum into deeper elastic territory.
learn.boundaryConditions: The formula MR = P(1 + 1/ε) requires that the firm faces the full demand curve — it does not apply to a competitive firm (which is a price taker with a horizontal demand curve, formally ε → −∞ and MR = P). The derivation assumes a smooth, differentiable demand curve; for kinked demand curves (as in some oligopoly models), MR has a discontinuity and the standard formula breaks at the kink.
examTraps: A student might think the MR curve has the same slope as the linear inverse demand curve, but the correct answer is that MR has twice the slope of the linear inverse demand — the MR curve is P = a − 2bQ when demand is P = a − bQ — so MR hits zero at Q = a/(2b), which is half the demand's Q-intercept.

A student might think a monopolist could profit by producing in the inelastic region (|ε| < 1), but the correct answer is that in the inelastic region MR < 0, so each additional unit reduces total revenue while adding to total cost — profit cannot be maximized there, and the monopolist will always reduce output to the elastic region.

A student might think TR is maximized where MC = 0, but the correct answer is that TR is maximized where MR = 0 (i.e., |ε| = 1) — if MC = 0, the profit-maximizing condition MR = MC = 0 coincides with the TR maximum, but this is a special case, not the general rule.

A student might forget that MR < P for a monopolist and use MR = P, but the correct answer is that MR = P only for a perfectly competitive firm (price taker) — for any downward-sloping demand, the monopolist must lower price on all units to sell one more, so MR = P(1 + 1/ε) < P since 1/ε < 0.

frq.prompt: A monopolist faces inverse demand P = 50 − Q and has constant marginal cost MC = 10.
parts: Derive the marginal revenue function and find the profit-maximizing quantity and price.
Calculate own-price elasticity at the profit-maximizing point and verify it is consistent with MR = MC using the formula MR = P(1 + 1/ε).
Find the TR-maximizing quantity and price, and explain why the profit-maximizing quantity differs from the TR-maximizing quantity.
Explain in words why a monopolist will never choose to produce in the inelastic region of demand, using both the MR formula and economic intuition about revenue and cost.

flashcards.front: Formula: Marginal revenue in terms of elasticity
flashcards.back: MR = P(1 + 1/ε). Since ε < 0 for downward-sloping demand, 1/ε < 0, so MR < P always. When |ε| > 1: MR > 0. When |ε| = 1: MR = 0. When |ε| < 1: MR < 0. This formula connects the revenue and elasticity concepts across all market structures.

flashcards.front: Formula: MR from linear inverse demand P = a − bQ
flashcards.back: MR = a − 2bQ. Same vertical intercept as demand (a), but twice the slope (−2b instead of −b). MR = 0 at Q = a/(2b), which is exactly half the demand curve's Q-intercept (a/b). This 'twice the slope' rule applies only to linear inverse demand.

flashcards.front: Formula: Total revenue and its derivative
flashcards.back: TR = P(Q)·Q. MR = dTR/dQ = P + Q(dP/dQ). The term Q(dP/dQ) is negative (demand slopes down), representing the revenue lost on existing units when price is cut to sell one more. This price-reduction cost is why MR < P for any firm with market power.

flashcards.front: Revenue rule: elastic vs. inelastic region
flashcards.back: Elastic (|ε| > 1): MR > 0 — cutting price raises TR because quantity gain outweighs price loss. Unit elastic (|ε| = 1): MR = 0 — TR is at maximum. Inelastic (|ε| < 1): MR < 0 — cutting price lowers TR because quantity gain is too small. A monopolist always prices in the elastic region.

flashcards.front: Why does a monopolist never produce in the inelastic region?
flashcards.back: In the inelastic region, MR < 0: selling an additional unit reduces total revenue. Since MC ≥ 0, profit = TR − TC would fall — the firm would strictly prefer to reduce output. Raising price (reducing Q) moves toward the elastic region, simultaneously raising TR and cutting TC, increasing profit unambiguously.

flashcards.front: Formula: TR-maximizing condition
flashcards.back: Set MR = 0: P(1 + 1/ε) = 0 → ε = −1 (unit elastic). Equivalently for linear demand, set a − 2bQ = 0 → Q* = a/(2b). TR maximum ≠ profit maximum unless MC = 0; with positive MC, profit maximization (MR = MC > 0) always occurs at a lower output than TR maximization.

flashcards.front: Graph: MR curve for linear inverse demand
flashcards.back: On a P-Q diagram: demand line runs from (0, a) to (a/b, 0). MR line runs from (0, a) — same intercept — to (a/2b, 0), hitting the Q-axis at exactly half the demand curve's intercept. The MR curve lies below and to the left of the demand curve for all Q > 0, reaching zero at the midpoint of the demand's Q-range.

flashcards.front: Distinction: MR for price taker vs. price maker
flashcards.back: Price taker (perfect competition): demand is horizontal, ε → −∞, so 1/ε → 0, and MR = P(1+0) = P. Price maker (monopoly/oligopoly): demand slopes down, ε is finite negative, MR = P(1+1/ε) < P. The formula MR = P(1+1/ε) unifies both cases and reveals that competition is the limit as |ε| → ∞.

flashcards.front: Formula: Lerner index derived from MR = MC
flashcards.back: At profit maximum: MR = MC → P(1+1/ε) = MC → (P−MC)/P = −1/ε = 1/|ε|. This is the Lerner index of market power: L = (P−MC)/P = 1/|ε|. More inelastic demand (lower |ε|) permits a larger markup. Perfect competition: |ε|→∞, L→0, P=MC.

flashcards.front: Formula: Verify elasticity from MR = P(1+1/ε)
flashcards.back: Given MR and P, solve for ε: 1/ε = (MR−P)/P = MR/P − 1, so ε = P/(MR−P). Or: 1+1/ε = MR/P, so 1/ε = MR/P − 1. This lets you recover the elasticity at any point if you know price and marginal revenue — a common exam verification step.

learn.openingTension: A market has thousands of buyers all wanting different quantities at different prices, and thousands of sellers with different cost structures. How does a single price emerge that clears the market — and once it does, how much total value has been created?
learn.coreIdea: Market equilibrium is the price P* at which quantity demanded exactly equals quantity supplied: Qd(P*) = Qs(P*). At this price no buyer is rationed and no seller has unsold inventory, so the market clears. The equilibrium also defines the total surplus — the aggregate net benefit to all participants — by measuring the areas between the demand and supply curves and the equilibrium price.
learn.mathSetup: Objective: find P* and Q* such that Qd(P*) = Qs(P*). Consumer Surplus: CS = ∫₀^{Q*} [D⁻¹(q) - P*] dq = area above P* and below the demand curve from 0 to Q*. Producer Surplus: PS = ∫₀^{Q*} [P* - S⁻¹(q)] dq = area below P* and above the supply curve from 0 to Q*. Total Surplus: TS = CS + PS = area between demand and supply curves from 0 to Q*.
learn.derivation:
1. Write demand as Qd = a - bP and supply as Qs = c + dP (with d > 0, b > 0). Economic reason: downward-sloping demand and upward-sloping supply are standard assumptions reflecting diminishing marginal willingness to pay and increasing marginal cost.
2. Set Qd = Qs: a - bP* = c + dP*. Economic reason: the equilibrium condition ensures no excess supply or excess demand.
3. Solve for P*: P* = (a - c)/(b + d). Economic reason: the equilibrium price balances both sides of the market; a higher choke price on demand or lower supply intercept raises P*.
4. Substitute P* back to get Q* = a - b·(a - c)/(b + d) = (ad + bc)/(b + d). Economic reason: equilibrium quantity rises when demand increases (higher a) or supply increases (higher c or d).
5. Compute CS = (1/2)(P_max - P*)·Q* where P_max = a/b is the demand choke price. Economic reason: CS is a triangle whose height is the gap between the highest willingness to pay and the market price.
6. Compute PS = (1/2)(P* - P_min)·Q* where P_min = -c/d is the supply choke price (minimum price at which any unit is supplied). Economic reason: PS is a triangle measuring seller gain above their minimum acceptable price.
learn.graphicalIntuition: Draw demand sloping down from left to right and supply sloping up. Where they cross is (Q*, P*). The triangle above P* and below the demand curve (from 0 to Q*) is CS — it captures the surplus buyers enjoy above what they pay. The triangle below P* and above the supply curve is PS — it captures producer surplus above their minimum. On a graph, total surplus is the entire lens-shaped area between the two curves from quantity 0 to Q*, and any restriction of trade to Q < Q* visibly shrinks this area — that lost area is deadweight loss. Common Misreading: students sometimes add CS and PS as separate triangles and double-count the rectangle P*·Q*. Remember: CS and PS together tile the area between demand and supply, not areas that include the revenue rectangle.
learn.workedExample: Given: Qd = 120 - 3P, Qs = -20 + 2P. Find: P*, Q*, CS, PS, TS. Solution: Set equal: 120 - 3P = -20 + 2P → 140 = 5P → P* = 28. Q* = 120 - 3(28) = 36. Demand choke price: P_max = 120/3 = 40. Supply choke price: P_min = 20/2 = 10. CS = (1/2)(40 - 28)(36) = (1/2)(12)(36) = 216. PS = (1/2)(28 - 10)(36) = (1/2)(18)(36) = 324. TS = 216 + 324 = 540. Interpret: At P* = 28, 36 units trade. Buyers collectively gain $216 above what they pay; sellers collectively gain $324 above their minimum supply price. Total economic value created by this market is $540.
learn.boundaryConditions: 1. If demand is perfectly inelastic (vertical demand curve), CS = 0 — all surplus is absorbed by sellers because buyers would pay any price for Q*. 2. If supply is perfectly elastic (horizontal supply curve), PS = 0 — all surplus goes to buyers because sellers are indifferent to price. 3. If government imposes a binding price floor above P*, the market no longer clears: Qs > Qd and the equilibrium concept breaks down — the actual quantity traded is Qd, not Qs. 4. With externalities, market equilibrium is not socially optimal — P* and Q* maximize private surplus but not total social welfare.
examTraps: A student might think total surplus equals price times quantity (P*·Q*), but the correct answer is total surplus is the area between the demand and supply curves from 0 to Q*, because P*·Q* is total revenue, not net surplus — surplus measures the gap between willingness to pay/accept and the actual price paid/received.

A student might think CS increases when P* rises (because price going up means buyers value goods more), but the correct answer is CS falls when P* rises, because the area above P* and below demand shrinks as the price wedge between P* and the choke price narrows.

A student might think equilibrium quantity is the quantity that maximizes CS alone, but the correct answer is equilibrium Q* maximizes total surplus CS + PS, and any other quantity is inefficient — this is the first welfare theorem.

A student might think the equilibrium changes immediately if a single buyer's preference shifts, but the correct answer is equilibrium reflects aggregate demand — a single buyer shifting has negligible effect in large markets; only a shift in the entire demand curve changes P* and Q*.

frq.prompt: In the market for used textbooks, demand is Qd = 80 - 2P and supply is Qs = -10 + P, where Q is hundreds of books and P is price in dollars.
parts: Find the equilibrium price P* and quantity Q*.
Calculate consumer surplus (CS) and producer surplus (PS) at the equilibrium. Show the formula and calculation for each.
Suppose a price ceiling of $25 is imposed. Calculate the new quantity traded and identify whether CS rises or falls relative to the free-market equilibrium.
Explain in words why a price ceiling set below equilibrium creates deadweight loss, even if it is intended to help buyers.

flashcards.front: What condition defines market equilibrium?
flashcards.back: Qd(P*) = Qs(P*) — quantity demanded equals quantity supplied at price P*, so the market clears with no excess supply or demand. This is the foundation for computing all welfare measures.

flashcards.front: Formula for Consumer Surplus (CS) with linear demand
flashcards.back: CS = (1/2)(P_max - P*)·Q*, where P_max is the demand choke price (willingness to pay of the last buyer if Q → 0). This triangle shrinks whenever P* rises or Q* falls.

flashcards.front: Formula for Producer Surplus (PS) with linear supply
flashcards.back: PS = (1/2)(P* - P_min)·Q*, where P_min is the supply choke price (lowest price at which any seller will produce). PS and CS together tile the area between demand and supply curves.

flashcards.front: Total Surplus = ?
flashcards.back: TS = CS + PS = area between demand and supply curves from 0 to Q*. This is maximized at competitive equilibrium — the first welfare theorem — and any deviation from Q* creates deadweight loss.

flashcards.front: Graph: how do you identify CS and PS on a supply-demand diagram?
flashcards.back: CS is the triangle above the horizontal price line P* and below the demand curve, bounded on the right at Q*. PS is the triangle below P* and above the supply curve, also bounded at Q*. Together they form the region between the two curves up to equilibrium.

flashcards.front: Distinction: total revenue vs. total surplus
flashcards.back: Total revenue = P*·Q* (what buyers pay, what sellers receive — these cancel in a closed market). Total surplus = (area between demand curve and P*) + (area between P* and supply curve) = net gains, not revenue. Students commonly confuse the two.

flashcards.front: What happens to total surplus if quantity traded is forced below Q*?
flashcards.back: TS falls — the units between the new quantity and Q* each had marginal value above marginal cost, so not trading them destroys net surplus. The lost area is deadweight loss, and no tax or transfer can recover it.

learn.openingTension: If a government levies a $5 tax on gasoline, who actually pays it — the gas station posting the price or the driver filling the tank? The legal answer (whoever writes the check) is completely different from the economic answer, and getting them confused leads to systematically wrong predictions about prices and quantities.
learn.coreIdea: A unit tax of $t per unit drives a wedge between the price buyers pay (Pb) and the price sellers receive (Ps): Pb - Ps = t. The equilibrium is found by satisfying both the demand-side condition Qd(Pb) = Q and the supply-side condition Qs(Ps) = Q simultaneously with the wedge constraint. Tax revenue equals t·Q*, and both CS and PS fall relative to the no-tax equilibrium.
learn.mathSetup: Wedge condition: Pb - Ps = t. Equilibrium conditions: Qd(Pb) = Qs(Ps) = Q*. Tax revenue: R = t·Q*. With linear demand Qd = a - bPb and supply Qs = c + dPs: substitute Ps = Pb - t into supply, set equal to demand. Solve for Pb, then Ps = Pb - t, then Q* = Qd(Pb).
learn.derivation:
1. Start with Qd = a - bPb and Qs = c + dPs. Economic reason: demand depends on what buyers pay; supply depends on what sellers receive — these are now different prices.
2. Impose wedge: Ps = Pb - t. Economic reason: every dollar the buyer pays, the seller gets one dollar less the tax, which the government collects.
3. Substitute into supply: Qs = c + d(Pb - t). Economic reason: supply shifts left (up) by exactly t — supply curve moves up by t in price space.
4. Set Qd = Qs: a - bPb = c + d(Pb - t). Rearrange: a - c + dt = (b + d)Pb. So Pb = (a - c + dt)/(b + d). Economic reason: buyer price rises relative to no-tax equilibrium by dt/(b+d).
5. Seller price: Ps = Pb - t = (a - c - bt)/(b + d). Economic reason: seller price falls by bt/(b+d). Note buyer price rise + seller price fall = t, confirming the wedge is exactly t.
6. New quantity: Q* = a - bPb = a - b(a - c + dt)/(b + d) = (ad + bc - bdt)/(b + d). Economic reason: Q* falls because the tax shrinks the effective gains from trade.
learn.graphicalIntuition: On a graph, imposing a unit tax shifts the supply curve up by exactly t (in price-axis terms) — the supply curve for what buyers pay moves up, while the supply curve for what sellers receive is unchanged. The new equilibrium has buyers on the original demand curve at Pb and sellers on the original supply curve at Ps, with Pb directly above Ps by the distance t. Tax revenue is the rectangle with height t and width Q* (tax-equilibrium quantity). CS is now the smaller triangle above Pb and below demand; PS is the smaller triangle below Ps and above supply. The area of lost CS + lost PS that is not captured as tax revenue is deadweight loss — a triangle between the old and new equilibrium quantities bounded by supply and demand. Common Misreading: students shift demand down by t instead of supply up by t (or vice versa) and get confused. Either shift gives the same Pb, Ps, Q* — the key is that supply and demand curves in buyer-price and seller-price space are different objects.
learn.workedExample: Given: Qd = 100 - 2P, Qs = -20 + 3P, unit tax t = $5 (collected from sellers). Find: Pb, Ps, Q*, tax revenue. Solution: With tax on sellers, Ps = Pb - 5. Substitute into supply: Qs = -20 + 3(Pb - 5) = -20 + 3Pb - 15 = -35 + 3Pb. Set equal to demand: 100 - 2Pb = -35 + 3Pb → 135 = 5Pb → Pb = 27. Ps = 27 - 5 = 22. Q* = 100 - 2(27) = 46. Pre-tax equilibrium: 100 - 2P = -20 + 3P → P* = 24, Q* = 52. Tax revenue = 5 × 46 = $230. Interpret: Buyers pay $27 (up $3 from $24), sellers receive $22 (down $2 from $24). The $5 tax is split: buyers bear $3, sellers bear $2. Quantity falls from 52 to 46.
learn.boundaryConditions: 1. If demand is perfectly inelastic (b = 0), Pb rises by the full t and Ps is unchanged — buyers bear 100% of the tax and quantity does not change, so there is no DWL but buyers lose exactly t·Q* in surplus. 2. If supply is perfectly elastic (d → ∞), Ps is unchanged and Pb rises by the full t — buyers again bear all burden, but now Q* falls proportionally with demand elasticity. 3. A tax collected from buyers vs. sellers produces identical Pb, Ps, and Q* — legal incidence is irrelevant to economic outcomes. 4. A subsidy (negative t) works symmetrically: Pb < Ps by the subsidy amount, Q* rises, and total surplus still falls because DWL from overproduction replaces DWL from underproduction.
examTraps: A student might think the side that legally pays the tax bears the full economic burden, but the correct answer is economic incidence depends only on relative elasticities — the legal payer is irrelevant, because market prices adjust until the wedge Pb - Ps = t is satisfied regardless of who writes the check.

A student might think tax revenue equals (Pb - Ps)·Q_old (using pre-tax quantity), but the correct answer is tax revenue = t·Q* where Q* is the new post-tax quantity, because fewer units are traded after the tax is imposed.

A student might think supply shifts down by t when a tax is imposed, but the correct answer is supply shifts up by t in price space (the curve sellers respond to is unchanged, but the curve showing buyer prices for any quantity shifts up by t), and the correct check is that Pb - Ps = t at the new equilibrium.

A student might think CS and PS losses from a tax simply transfer to the government as revenue, but the correct answer is only t·Q* transfers to the government — the additional CS and PS loss beyond that is deadweight loss that no one captures.

frq.prompt: The market for hotel rooms has demand Qd = 200 - 4P and supply Qs = -40 + 6P (Q in rooms, P in dollars per night). The city imposes a unit tax of $10 per room per night, collected from hotels.
parts: Find the pre-tax equilibrium price and quantity.
Find the post-tax buyer price Pb, seller price Ps, and equilibrium quantity Q*. Show all steps using the wedge condition.
Calculate tax revenue and identify how much of the original $10 tax is borne by buyers vs. sellers.
Explain in words why it does not matter whether the city collects the tax from hotels or from guests, and what economic principle this illustrates.

flashcards.front: What is the wedge condition for a unit tax?
flashcards.back: Pb - Ps = t, where Pb is the buyer price, Ps is the seller price, and t is the unit tax. This single equation, combined with Qd(Pb) = Qs(Ps), pins the post-tax equilibrium.

flashcards.front: How does a unit tax shift the supply curve on a graph?
flashcards.back: In buyer-price space, the supply curve shifts up by exactly t — for every quantity, buyers must now pay t more to make sellers willing to supply that amount. The original supply curve (in seller-price terms) does not move.

flashcards.front: Formula for tax revenue
flashcards.back: Tax revenue R = t · Q*, where Q* is the post-tax equilibrium quantity (not the pre-tax quantity). Because the tax reduces quantity, revenue is always less than t · Q_pre-tax.

flashcards.front: Distinction: legal incidence vs. economic incidence of a tax
flashcards.back: Legal incidence is who writes the check to the government. Economic incidence is how the tax burden is shared through price changes — determined by elasticities, not by legal designation. They can differ completely.

flashcards.front: What happens to CS, PS, and total surplus when a unit tax is imposed?
flashcards.back: CS falls (buyers pay more), PS falls (sellers receive less), and the government collects revenue t·Q*. The remaining loss — lost CS + lost PS not offset by revenue — is deadweight loss, so total surplus including government = TS_pre - DWL.

flashcards.front: If Qd = a - bP and Qs = c + dP, what is the buyer price Pb after a unit tax t?
flashcards.back: Pb = (a - c + dt)/(b + d). The buyer price rises by dt/(b+d) relative to P*. Seller price Ps = Pb - t = (a - c - bt)/(b+d), falling by bt/(b+d). Together the rise and fall sum to t.

flashcards.front: Condition: when does a unit tax create no deadweight loss?
flashcards.back: When either demand or supply is perfectly inelastic — the quantity traded does not change, so no mutually beneficial trades are lost. However, surplus is still redistributed (the inelastic side bears the full burden), so DWL = 0 does not mean the tax is harmless.

learn.openingTension: A sales tax of 8% and a $2-per-unit tax both raise government revenue, but they behave differently when prices are high vs. low. Why does a percentage-based tax automatically collect more revenue in expensive markets, and when can you safely treat the two as equivalent?
learn.coreIdea: An ad valorem tax is a tax levied as a fraction τ of the transaction price rather than a fixed dollar amount per unit. If the buyer pays P, the seller receives P(1 - τ) and the government collects τP. This is equivalent to a unit tax only at a specific price level — at equilibrium, an ad valorem tax of τ acts like a unit tax of t = τP*, so for small τ the two are approximately interchangeable. VAT and most retail sales taxes are ad valorem; excise taxes on cigarettes and fuel are often unit taxes.
learn.mathSetup: Buyer price: Pb = P. Seller price: Ps = P(1 - τ). Wedge: Pb - Ps = τP = τPb. So the wedge is proportional to the buyer price, not fixed. Equivalent unit tax at equilibrium: t_equiv = τP*. For linear demand Qd = a - bP and supply Qs = c + dPs: substitute Ps = P(1-τ) into supply and solve Qd(P) = Qs(P(1-τ)).
learn.derivation:
1. Write supply as function of seller price: Qs = c + dPs. Economic reason: sellers respond to what they actually receive after tax.
2. Substitute Ps = P(1 - τ): Qs = c + dP(1 - τ). Economic reason: seller's effective supply curve rotates — at higher prices the tax wedge is larger, so supply rotates inward rather than shifting in parallel.
3. Set demand equal to effective supply: a - bP = c + dP(1 - τ). Rearrange: a - c = P[b + d(1 - τ)]. So P* = (a - c)/[b + d(1 - τ)]. Economic reason: because the wedge grows with price, the denominator shrinks less than in the unit-tax case for the same revenue target.
4. Seller price: Ps = P*(1 - τ). Economic reason: seller receives only the fraction (1 - τ) of whatever the market price settles at.
5. Quantity: Q* = a - bP*. Economic reason: quantity depends on what buyers pay, which is P*, the posted market price.
6. Compare to unit tax: if τ is small, τP* ≈ t for a unit tax calibrated to the same equilibrium. The approximation breaks down at large τ because the ad valorem tax's wedge grows nonlinearly with price.
learn.graphicalIntuition: On a graph, a unit tax shifts the supply curve up by a constant vertical distance t at every quantity. An ad valorem tax rotates the supply curve — the vertical distance between the original supply curve and the taxed supply curve is τP, which is larger at higher prices and smaller at lower prices. This means the ad valorem supply curve is steeper than the original. On a graph, the two taxes produce different equilibria unless τP* = t exactly. Common Misreading: students draw the ad valorem tax as a parallel upward shift of supply (like a unit tax). The correct picture is a rotation: the tax line and the original supply line converge at the supply-axis intercept (where P = 0 and the tax = 0).
learn.workedExample: Given: Qd = 80 - 2P, Qs = -10 + 3P, ad valorem tax τ = 0.10 (10%), collected from sellers. Find: equilibrium P, Q, tax revenue. Solution: Seller receives Ps = P(1 - 0.10) = 0.9P. Effective supply: Qs = -10 + 3(0.9P) = -10 + 2.7P. Set equal to demand: 80 - 2P = -10 + 2.7P → 90 = 4.7P → P* = 19.15 (≈ $19.15). Q* = 80 - 2(19.15) = 41.70. Ps = 0.9 × 19.15 = $17.23. Tax per unit = 19.15 - 17.23 = $1.91. Tax revenue = 1.91 × 41.70 ≈ $79.80. Equivalent unit tax at this price: τP* = 0.10 × 19.15 = $1.915 ≈ $1.91. Interpret: The ad valorem tax acts exactly like a unit tax of $1.91 at this equilibrium price. Pre-tax equilibrium: 80 - 2P = -10 + 3P → P = 18, Q = 44. Buyer price rose by $1.15; seller price fell by $0.77.
learn.boundaryConditions: 1. When τ = 0, there is no tax and the market is at the competitive equilibrium — no wedge exists. 2. When τ = 1 (100% tax), sellers receive nothing and supply collapses to zero — no trade occurs. 3. The equivalence ad valorem ≈ unit tax holds only at a specific price; if the pre-tax equilibrium price changes (e.g., demand shifts), the two taxes produce different new equilibria because the ad valorem tax wedge changes automatically while the unit tax does not. 4. If the market has multiple equilibria (e.g., with network effects), the equivalence may hold locally but break down globally.
examTraps: A student might think an ad valorem tax shifts the supply curve up by a constant amount (like a unit tax), but the correct answer is it rotates the supply curve — the vertical distance between taxed and untaxed supply equals τP, which grows with price, making the taxed supply curve steeper.

A student might think ad valorem and unit taxes are always equivalent, but the correct answer is they are equivalent only when calibrated to the same equilibrium price τP* = t; after any market shock, the two taxes diverge because the ad valorem wedge automatically adjusts to the new price while the unit tax does not.

A student might think the tax rate τ tells you directly how much revenue is collected per unit, but the correct answer is revenue per unit = τ·P (not τ alone), so the same τ raises more revenue in a high-price market than a low-price market — this is why governments often prefer ad valorem taxes on luxury goods.

A student might think who collects the ad valorem tax (buyer or seller) changes the equilibrium, but the correct answer is only the wedge Pb/Ps = 1/(1-τ) matters, not which side remits the tax — the economic incidence is determined by elasticities, just as with unit taxes.

frq.prompt: A state levies a 15% ad valorem sales tax (τ = 0.15) on restaurant meals. Market demand is Qd = 100 - 5P and supply is Qs = -20 + 4P (Q in thousands of meals per week, P in dollars).
parts: Find the pre-tax equilibrium price and quantity.
Write the effective supply equation after the tax and find the new equilibrium price P* and quantity Q*.
Calculate tax revenue and the effective unit tax equivalent at this equilibrium.

Explain why an ad valorem tax automatically collects more revenue if demand rises (shifting the demand curve right), while a unit tax of the same initial value would not — and what this implies for government revenue stability.

flashcards.front: Definition: ad valorem tax
flashcards.back: A tax levied as a fraction τ of the transaction price, so the wedge between buyer and seller price equals τP — not a fixed dollar amount. VAT and retail sales taxes are the most common examples.

flashcards.front: Ad valorem wedge formula
flashcards.back: Pb - Ps = τPb, so Ps = Pb(1 - τ). Unlike a unit tax where the wedge is constant, here the wedge grows with the price level, causing the effective supply curve to rotate rather than shift in parallel.

flashcards.front: How does an ad valorem tax shift the supply curve graphically?
flashcards.back: It rotates the supply curve upward — the vertical distance between the original and taxed supply curves equals τP at each price, which is zero when P = 0 and grows as P rises. A unit tax, by contrast, shifts the curve up by a constant distance t.

flashcards.front: When is an ad valorem tax equivalent to a unit tax?
flashcards.back: At equilibrium, an ad valorem tax τ is equivalent to a unit tax t = τP*. This equivalence holds only at that specific equilibrium price — after any demand or supply shock, the two taxes diverge because the ad valorem wedge adjusts automatically while the unit tax stays fixed.

flashcards.front: Distinction: unit tax vs. ad valorem tax when prices change
flashcards.back: A unit tax collects the same dollar amount per unit regardless of market price changes. An ad valorem tax collects a fixed fraction of price, so revenue rises automatically when market prices rise. This makes ad valorem taxes more revenue-stable over time.

flashcards.front: Formula for equilibrium price under ad valorem tax τ, with Qd = a - bP and Qs = c + dP
flashcards.back: P* = (a - c) / [b + d(1 - τ)]. As τ rises, the denominator shrinks, so P* rises. Seller receives Ps = P*(1 - τ). This parallels the unit-tax formula but with the demand-slope term multiplied by (1 - τ) on the supply side.

learn.openingTension: Congress passes a payroll tax officially paid by employers. Workers assume they get off free. But within a year, economists find that wages have fallen nearly as much as the tax. Who really paid? This gap between legal obligation and economic reality is one of the most important — and most misunderstood — ideas in public finance.
learn.coreIdea: Tax incidence measures who bears the economic burden of a tax — meaning who actually experiences a reduction in purchasing power — as opposed to who legally remits payment. Economic incidence is determined entirely by the relative price elasticities of supply and demand: the more inelastic side of the market has fewer alternatives and therefore absorbs more of the price wedge. The legal side that writes the check to the government is economically irrelevant because prices adjust until both sides face the same wedge.
learn.mathSetup: Buyer's share of tax burden: ΔPb / t = ε_s / (ε_s - ε_d). Seller's share: -ΔPs / t = -ε_d / (ε_s - ε_d). Note: ε_d < 0 so -ε_d > 0, and ε_s > 0, so both shares are positive and sum to 1. Buyer's burden in dollars: t · ε_s/(ε_s - ε_d). Seller's burden in dollars: t · (-ε_d)/(ε_s - ε_d). Total burden = t (the full tax is borne by market participants).
learn.derivation:
1. Start with Pb = P* + ΔPb and Ps = P* - ΔPs. Economic reason: relative to pre-tax equilibrium, buyers pay more and sellers receive less.
2. Require ΔPb + ΔPs = t (wedge sums to t). Economic reason: the full tax must be accounted for — every cent of tax comes from either buyers paying more or sellers receiving less.
3. At equilibrium, %ΔQd = ε_d · %ΔPb and %ΔQs = ε_s · %ΔPs. Economic reason: elasticities measure how much quantity changes per percentage change in the relevant price.
4. Require ΔQd = ΔQs = ΔQ (market still clears after tax). Economic reason: equilibrium requires supply equals demand even after the tax — the quantity constraint ties buyer and seller price changes together.
5. From the equal-quantity condition: ε_d · (ΔPb/P*) = ε_s · (ΔPs/P*). Divide both sides: ε_d · ΔPb = ε_s · ΔPs. Also ΔPb + ΔPs = t. Solve: ΔPb = t · ε_s/(ε_s - ε_d); ΔPs = t · (-ε_d)/(ε_s - ε_d).
6. Interpret: buyer's share = ε_s/(ε_s - ε_d). As |ε_d| → 0 (inelastic demand), buyer share → 1 (buyers bear all). As ε_s → 0 (inelastic supply), buyer share → 0 (sellers bear all). Economic reason: the inelastic side cannot substitute away from the tax, so they absorb it.
learn.graphicalIntuition: On a graph, draw demand and supply crossing at (Q*, P*). After a tax, the new equilibrium has buyers at Pb (higher) and sellers at Ps (lower), with vertical distance t between them. The buyer's share is the distance (Pb - P*) as a fraction of t; the seller's share is (P* - Ps) as a fraction of t. The steeper (more inelastic) curve determines which side moves less in quantity and therefore more in price — the inelastic side cannot escape the tax through quantity adjustment. On a graph, if demand is nearly vertical (inelastic), Pb rises almost the full t and Ps barely changes — buyers bear almost all the burden. If supply is nearly vertical, Ps falls almost the full t. Common Misreading: students think the side that legally pays the tax is represented by the curve that 'moves' on the graph. Neither curve moves in seller-price/buyer-price space — it is the equilibrium that moves along both curves simultaneously.
learn.workedExample: Given: Qd = 100 - 2P, Qs = -20 + 3P, unit tax t = $5. Find: pre-tax equilibrium, post-tax Pb and Ps, each side's burden, tax revenue, and incidence shares. Solution — Pre-tax equilibrium: 100 - 2P = -20 + 3P → 120 = 5P → P* = 24. Q* = 100 - 2(24) = 52. Post-tax: impose wedge Pb - Ps = 5. Sellers face Ps = Pb - 5. Substitute into supply: Qs = -20 + 3(Pb - 5) = -20 + 3Pb - 15 = -35 + 3Pb. Set equal to demand: 100 - 2Pb = -35 + 3Pb → 135 = 5Pb → Pb = 27. Ps = 27 - 5 = 22. Q* = 100 - 2(27) = 46. Buyer's burden: ΔPb = 27 - 24 = $3. Seller's burden: ΔPs = 24 - 22 = $2. Check: $3 + $2 = $5 = t. Tax revenue: R = 5 × 46 = $230. Incidence check via elasticity formula: at pre-tax P* = 24, Q* = 52. ε_d = (dQd/dP)·(P*/Q*) = (-2)·(24/52) = -0.923. ε_s = (dQs/dP)·(P*/Q*) = (3)·(24/52) = 1.385. Buyer share = ε_s/(ε_s - ε_d) = 1.385/(1.385 - (-0.923)) = 1.385/2.308 = 0.600. Buyer's $3 burden / $5 tax = 0.60. Seller share = 0.923/2.308 = 0.400. Seller's $2 burden / $5 tax = 0.40. Interpret: Buyers bear 60% ($3) and sellers bear 40% ($2) of the $5 tax. Demand is more inelastic than supply at this equilibrium (|ε_d| = 0.923 < ε_s = 1.385), so buyers — the less elastic side — bear the larger share.
learn.boundaryConditions: 1. Perfectly inelastic demand (ε_d = 0): buyer share = ε_s/(ε_s - 0) = 1 — buyers bear 100%, quantity is unchanged, no DWL. 2. Perfectly elastic demand (ε_d → -∞): buyer share → 0 — sellers bear 100%, quantity falls dramatically, large DWL. 3. Perfectly inelastic supply (ε_s = 0): buyer share = 0/(0 - ε_d) = 0 — sellers bear 100%, quantity unchanged, no DWL. 4. Perfectly elastic supply (ε_s → ∞): buyer share → 1 — buyers bear 100%, quantity falls, DWL exists. 5. The legal assignment of who pays the tax is irrelevant — switching the legal payer from buyers to sellers does not change Pb, Ps, Q*, or incidence shares.
examTraps: A student might think the side that legally pays the tax bears the entire economic burden, but the correct answer is economic incidence depends only on supply and demand elasticities — legal incidence is irrelevant because market prices adjust to split the burden according to the elasticity ratio.

A student might think an inelastic demand means buyers pay less of the tax (because they buy less), but the correct answer is inelastic demand means buyers bear more of the tax — because they cannot reduce quantity much, the price must rise substantially on the buyer side to maintain the wedge.

A student might think buyer's share + seller's share can exceed 1 (e.g., if both are hurt badly), but the correct answer is the shares always sum to exactly 1 — the full tax burden t is divided between buyers and sellers with none left over or double-counted, as verified by ΔPb + ΔPs = t.

A student might think the elasticity-based incidence formula requires knowing the quantity change, but the correct answer is the shares depend only on the ratio ε_s/(ε_s - ε_d) and -ε_d/(ε_s - ε_d) — you need elasticities at the pre-tax equilibrium, not the actual quantity change.

frq.prompt: The market for cigarettes has demand Qd = 200 - 10P and supply Qs = -40 + 5P (Q in millions of packs per year, P in dollars per pack). The government imposes a $3 per-pack unit tax collected from manufacturers.
parts: Find the pre-tax equilibrium price P* and quantity Q*. Calculate price elasticity of demand and price elasticity of supply at the equilibrium.
Find the post-tax buyer price Pb, seller price Ps, and quantity Q*_tax. Show all steps using the wedge condition.
Calculate the buyer's and seller's share of the tax burden using both the direct price-change method and the elasticity formula. Verify they are consistent.
Explain why the fact that manufacturers write the tax check to the government does not mean they bear the full economic burden, and what elasticity comparison determines who bears more.

flashcards.front: Definition: tax incidence
flashcards.back: Tax incidence is the distribution of the actual economic burden of a tax across buyers and sellers — determined by elasticities, not by who legally remits payment to the government. It measures changes in real purchasing power, not cash flows.

flashcards.front: Buyer's share of tax burden formula
flashcards.back: Buyer's share = ε_s / (ε_s - ε_d), where ε_s > 0 and ε_d < 0. This fraction is between 0 and 1. A higher supply elasticity (more elastic supply) means buyers bear more; a higher |ε_d| (more elastic demand) means buyers bear less.

flashcards.front: Seller's share of tax burden formula
flashcards.back: Seller's share = -ε_d / (ε_s - ε_d). Since ε_d < 0, -ε_d > 0. Buyer's share + seller's share = [ε_s + (-ε_d)] / (ε_s - ε_d) = 1. The full tax is always borne by market participants.

flashcards.front: Rule: which side bears more of a tax?
flashcards.back: The more inelastic side bears the larger share of any tax. If |ε_d| < ε_s, buyers bear more (demand is more inelastic). If |ε_d| > ε_s, sellers bear more (supply is more inelastic). Equal elasticities mean a 50/50 split.

flashcards.front: Worked numbers: Qd = 100 - 2P, Qs = -20 + 3P, t = $5. What are Pb, Ps, and Q*?
flashcards.back: Pb = 27, Ps = 22, Q* = 46. Buyers pay $3 more than pre-tax P* = 24; sellers receive $2 less. Buyer bears 60% (3/5), seller bears 40% (2/5) of the $5 tax. Tax revenue = $230.

flashcards.front: Incidence check: Qd = 100 - 2P, Qs = -20 + 3P at P* = 24, Q* = 52. What are ε_d and ε_s?
Condition: perfectly inelastic demand — who bears the tax?
flashcards.back: Buyers bear 100% of the tax (buyer share = ε_s/(ε_s - 0) = 1). Quantity does not change, there is no deadweight loss, and the entire tax is a transfer from buyers to the government. This is the maximum buyer burden case.

flashcards.front: Condition: perfectly inelastic supply — who bears the tax?
flashcards.back: Sellers bear 100% of the tax (buyer share = 0/(0 - ε_d) = 0). Quantity does not change, there is no deadweight loss, and the entire tax is a transfer from sellers to the government. Land taxation is the classic example.

flashcards.front: Condition: perfectly elastic demand — who bears the tax?
flashcards.back: Sellers bear 100% of the tax — buyers refuse to pay a higher price, so the full wedge is absorbed by sellers through a lower Ps. Quantity falls substantially and there is large deadweight loss.

flashcards.front: Condition: perfectly elastic supply — who bears the tax?
flashcards.back: Buyers bear 100% of the tax — sellers will not accept a lower price, so Pb rises by the full tax amount. Quantity falls and deadweight loss occurs. This is the long-run competitive market case for a constant-cost industry.

flashcards.front: Distinction: legal incidence vs. economic incidence in practice
flashcards.back: Payroll taxes nominally split 50/50 between employer and employee, but economists find workers bear most of the burden through lower wages because labor supply is inelastic relative to labor demand — a textbook example of legal incidence diverging from economic incidence.

learn.openingTension: A tax raises $100 million for the government but destroys $30 million in economic value that no one captures. That $30 million is deadweight loss — not a transfer to anyone, but a permanent destruction of gains from trade. Why does it happen, and what determines how large it is?
learn.coreIdea: Deadweight loss (DWL) is the net loss in total surplus from a tax that is not offset by gains to any party — it represents trades that would have been mutually beneficial but no longer occur because the tax drives a wedge between buyer and seller prices. DWL equals the area of the triangle between the demand and supply curves bounded by the old and new equilibrium quantities. It rises with the square of the tax rate and with the elasticities of both supply and demand.
learn.mathSetup: DWL = (1/2) · |t| · |ΔQ|, where ΔQ = Q*_post-tax - Q*_pre-tax < 0. Equivalently: DWL = (1/2) · t² · [ε_d · ε_s / (ε_s - ε_d)] · (Q*/P*). Note the t² term: doubling the tax quadruples DWL, while only doubling revenue. This is the Ramsey inverse-elasticity rule's core motivation.
learn.derivation:
1. Identify pre-tax equilibrium (P*, Q_0) and post-tax equilibrium quantities Q_1 (where Q_1 < Q_0). Economic reason: the tax reduces the number of units traded by making some trades no longer mutually beneficial.
2. The lost units are those between Q_1 and Q_0. For each such unit, the buyer's marginal willingness to pay (on the demand curve) exceeds the seller's marginal cost (on the supply curve). Economic reason: these trades would generate positive surplus but the tax wedge prevents them.
3. DWL = ∫_{Q_1}^{Q_0} [D⁻¹(q) - S⁻¹(q)] dq. With linear supply and demand, D⁻¹ - S⁻¹ is linear in q and this integral equals a triangle. Economic reason: the gap between demand and supply prices narrows linearly from t (at Q_1) to 0 (at Q_0).
4. Triangle area = (1/2) · base · height = (1/2) · |ΔQ| · t. Economic reason: base is the quantity reduction; height is the tax wedge t (the gap at the new equilibrium).
5. Use elasticity to express ΔQ: ΔQ = [ε_d · ε_s / (ε_s - ε_d)] · (Q*/P*) · t. Economic reason: higher elasticities mean larger quantity response to the same price wedge, so DWL grows with elasticities.
6. Substitute to get DWL = (1/2) · t² · [ε_d · ε_s / (ε_s - ε_d)] · (Q*/P*). Note t² — this means DWL rises faster than revenue (which grows roughly linearly in t), so large taxes are disproportionately costly.
learn.graphicalIntuition: On a graph, place pre-tax equilibrium at (Q_0, P*) where demand and supply cross. After the tax, buyers pay Pb (on demand curve) and sellers receive Ps (on supply curve), with Pb - Ps = t. The tax revenue rectangle has height t and width Q_1 (new quantity). The deadweight loss is the triangle with vertices at (Q_1, Pb), (Q_1, Ps), and (Q_0, P*). Its base is the quantity reduction Q_0 - Q_1 and its height is t. On a graph, DWL is clearly the piece of the original total surplus triangle (between supply and demand, right of Q_1) that is neither CS, PS, nor tax revenue — it is lost to everyone. Common Misreading: students sometimes include the tax revenue rectangle in DWL. The tax revenue is not lost — it goes to the government — only the triangle beyond the revenue rectangle is deadweight loss.
learn.workedExample: Given: Qd = 100 - 2P, Qs = -20 + 3P, unit tax t = $5. Find: pre-tax equilibrium, post-tax equilibrium, quantity change, and exact DWL triangle. Solution — Pre-tax: 100 - 2P = -20 + 3P → P* = 24, Q_0 = 52. Post-tax (from t78 worked example): Pb = 27, Ps = 22, Q_1 = 46. Quantity change: ΔQ = 46 - 52 = -6 units. DWL triangle: base = |ΔQ| = 6, height = t = 5. DWL = (1/2) · 6 · 5 = (1/2) · 30 = $15. Verify with elasticity formula: ε_d = -0.923, ε_s = 1.385 (from t78). DWL = (1/2) · 25 · [(-0.923)(1.385)/(1.385 - (-0.923))] · (52/24) = (1/2) · 25 · [(-1.279)/2.308] · 2.167. Note: use |ε_d · ε_s / (ε_s - ε_d)| = (0.923)(1.385)/2.308 = 0.554. DWL = (1/2) · 25 · 0.554 · 2.167 = (1/2) · 25 · 1.200 = $15.00. Both methods confirm DWL = $15. Interpret: Of the pre-tax total surplus, $230 is captured as tax revenue, and $15 is destroyed — the 6 units that would have traded at prices between $22 and $27 no longer occur, each losing surplus equal to the gap between willingness to pay and supply cost.
learn.boundaryConditions: 1. Perfectly inelastic demand or supply (ε = 0 on either side): |ΔQ| = 0, so DWL = 0. Revenue is maximized for a given welfare cost in this case — Ramsey taxation targets inelastic goods for this reason. 2. Perfectly elastic demand or supply (|ε| → ∞): DWL grows without bound for any finite tax — all trades are destroyed because quantity collapses completely. 3. DWL scales with t² while revenue scales roughly with t, so the marginal DWL per dollar of revenue rises with the tax rate — a small tax is relatively efficient; a large tax is disproportionately costly. 4. With externalities, the pre-tax market is already inefficient and a Pigouvian tax set equal to the external cost can reduce DWL rather than creating it.
examTraps: A student might think deadweight loss equals the total drop in CS plus the total drop in PS, but the correct answer is DWL = drop in CS + drop in PS - tax revenue, because the tax revenue is transferred to the government and not destroyed — only the triangle not captured by anyone is DWL.

A student might think that doubling the tax doubles the deadweight loss, but the correct answer is DWL scales with t², so doubling the tax quadruples DWL — this is why high tax rates are disproportionately harmful to efficiency relative to the revenue they raise.

A student might think elastic supply or demand reduces DWL because the tax raises less revenue, but the correct answer is higher elasticities increase DWL — more elastic markets have larger quantity reductions in response to the tax wedge, creating a larger triangle of destroyed surplus.

A student might think DWL exists only if the tax is collected from the 'wrong' side, but the correct answer is DWL arises from the price wedge itself, regardless of which side legally pays — the economic distortion is the same whether buyers or sellers remit the tax.

frq.prompt: Return to the market with Qd = 100 - 2P and Qs = -20 + 3P. The government imposes a $5 unit tax.
parts: Calculate the pre-tax and post-tax equilibrium quantities. What is the change in quantity traded?
Calculate deadweight loss using the triangle formula DWL = (1/2) · t · |ΔQ|. Draw and label the DWL triangle on a supply-demand diagram (describe the vertices).
If the tax were doubled to $10, predict the new DWL. Explain why DWL does not simply double.
Explain why a Pigouvian tax on a negative externality can reduce rather than create deadweight loss, using the concept of DWL and social vs. private surplus.

flashcards.front: Definition: deadweight loss from a tax
flashcards.back: Deadweight loss is the reduction in total surplus from a tax that is not captured by any party — it represents mutually beneficial trades that no longer occur because the tax wedge makes them unprofitable for at least one side. It is purely destroyed value.

flashcards.front: DWL triangle formula
flashcards.back: DWL = (1/2) · |t| · |ΔQ|, where t is the tax per unit and ΔQ = Q_post - Q_pre < 0. This is the area of the triangle between supply and demand curves, bounded by the old and new equilibrium quantities.

flashcards.front: DWL formula in terms of elasticities
flashcards.back: DWL = (1/2) · t² · |ε_d · ε_s / (ε_s - ε_d)| · (Q*/P*). The t² term means doubling the tax quadruples DWL. Higher elasticities (on either side) increase DWL by increasing the quantity reduction.

flashcards.front: Worked result: Qd = 100 - 2P, Qs = -20 + 3P, t = $5. What is the DWL?
flashcards.back: DWL = (1/2)(5)(6) = $15. Pre-tax Q = 52, post-tax Q = 46, so ΔQ = 6 units lost. Tax revenue = 5 × 46 = $230. DWL triangle has base 6 (quantity units lost) and height $5 (tax wedge).

flashcards.front: Graph: describe the DWL triangle vertices
flashcards.back: The DWL triangle has three vertices: (Q_post, Pb) on the demand curve, (Q_post, Ps) on the supply curve, and (Q_pre, P*) where supply and demand intersect. Base = Q_pre - Q_post (horizontal), height = t = Pb - Ps (vertical). Tax revenue rectangle is to the left of this triangle.

flashcards.front: How does DWL scale with the tax rate?
flashcards.back: DWL scales with t² (tax rate squared), while revenue scales roughly with t. So doubling the tax doubles revenue but quadruples DWL. This is why the marginal efficiency cost of taxation rises with the tax rate.

flashcards.front: Condition: when is DWL zero despite a positive tax?
flashcards.back: DWL = 0 when either supply or demand is perfectly inelastic, because |ΔQ| = 0 — no trades are eliminated by the tax. Revenue is maximized per unit of welfare cost in this case, which is why Ramsey optimal taxation targets inelastic goods.

flashcards.front: Distinction: tax revenue vs. deadweight loss
flashcards.back: Tax revenue (t · Q*) is a transfer from buyers and sellers to the government — it remains inside the economy. DWL is not transferred anywhere; it is surplus that simply ceases to exist because trades that would have happened at prices between Ps and Pb no longer occur.

flashcards.front: Condition: when does a tax reduce rather than create deadweight loss?
flashcards.back: When there is a negative externality — the pre-tax market overproduces relative to the social optimum. A Pigouvian tax equal to the external marginal cost moves quantity toward Q_social, reducing the externality-driven DWL rather than creating new DWL.

flashcards.front: Relationship between elasticity and DWL
flashcards.back: Higher elasticity on either side (supply or demand) increases DWL for a given tax, because elastic agents reduce quantity more in response to the price wedge — more trades are eliminated. This is why inelastic goods are more efficient tax bases.

learn.openingTension: If governments want to minimize economic damage when raising revenue, should they tax goods with elastic or inelastic demand? And if a special interest group claims a tax on their industry falls entirely on consumers, what elasticity relationship would make that literally true?
learn.coreIdea: The elasticity-based incidence formulas make four extreme cases exact and policy-relevant: perfectly inelastic demand means buyers bear 100% of any tax; perfectly elastic demand means sellers bear 100%; perfectly inelastic supply means sellers bear 100%; perfectly elastic supply means buyers bear 100%. These are not approximations — they are exact results that follow directly from the wedge condition and the elasticity definition. Understanding them reveals why property taxes fall on landowners and why payroll taxes often fall on workers.
learn.mathSetup: Buyer's tax burden: ΔPb = t · ε_s / (ε_s - ε_d). Seller's tax burden: -ΔPs = t · (-ε_d) / (ε_s - ε_d). Four limiting cases: (1) ε_d → 0: ΔPb → t, ΔPs → 0. (2) ε_d → -∞: ΔPb → 0, ΔPs → -t. (3) ε_s → 0: ΔPb → 0, ΔPs → -t. (4) ε_s → +∞: ΔPb → t, ΔPs → 0.
learn.derivation:
1. Recall: buyer share = ε_s / (ε_s - ε_d). Economic reason: this ratio measures how much of the tax-induced price change is forced onto buyers by the inelasticity of demand relative to supply.
2. Case 1 — perfectly inelastic demand (ε_d = 0): buyer share = ε_s / (ε_s - 0) = 1. Economic reason: buyers must purchase the same quantity regardless of price, so sellers can pass the full tax forward as a price increase.
3. Case 2 — perfectly elastic demand (ε_d → -∞): buyer share = ε_s / (ε_s - (-∞)) = 0. Economic reason: at the slightest price increase, buyers disappear entirely — sellers must absorb the full tax in lower revenues to keep buyers at all.
4. Case 3 — perfectly inelastic supply (ε_s = 0): buyer share = 0 / (0 - ε_d) = 0. Economic reason: sellers supply the same amount regardless of price, so buyers can refuse to pay more — sellers must absorb the full wedge as a reduction in Ps. Classic example: tax on fixed land.
5. Case 4 — perfectly elastic supply (ε_s → +∞): buyer share = ∞ / (∞ - ε_d) = 1. Economic reason: sellers will not produce at any price below their constant marginal cost — buyers must pay the full tax in a higher price or go without. Classic example: long-run competitive market with constant costs.
6. Continuous case: for intermediate elasticities, the share varies smoothly between 0 and 1, and the side with the lower absolute elasticity bears the larger share — intuition confirmed by the formula.
learn.graphicalIntuition: On a graph, draw a vertical demand curve (perfectly inelastic) and a tax imposed on sellers. The supply curve shifts up by t, but because demand is vertical, the quantity is fixed and Pb rises by exactly t while Ps is unchanged — buyers bear everything. Now redraw with a horizontal demand curve (perfectly elastic): the supply shift cannot raise the buyer price at all because buyers leave immediately at any higher price; instead Ps falls by t — sellers bear everything. Perfectly inelastic supply (vertical supply) means Ps must fall by t because the same quantity reaches the market regardless; perfectly elastic supply (horizontal supply) means Pb must rise by t because sellers refuse to supply below their minimum price. On a graph, the shape of the curve — vertical vs. horizontal — immediately reveals who bears the tax.
learn.workedExample: Given: market with supply elasticity ε_s = 2 and demand elasticity ε_d = -0.5, unit tax t = $10. Find: buyer and seller burden, and verify these are consistent with 'demand is more inelastic.' Solution: |ε_d| = 0.5 < ε_s = 2, so demand is more inelastic — buyers should bear more. Buyer share = ε_s/(ε_s - ε_d) = 2/(2 - (-0.5)) = 2/2.5 = 0.80. Buyer burden = 0.80 × $10 = $8. Seller share = -ε_d/(ε_s - ε_d) = 0.5/2.5 = 0.20. Seller burden = 0.20 × $10 = $2. Check: $8 + $2 = $10 = t. Interpret: Buyers bear 80% of the $10 tax because their demand elasticity (0.5 in absolute value) is much lower than supply elasticity (2). They have fewer alternatives when price rises, so sellers successfully pass most of the tax forward.
learn.boundaryConditions: 1. If ε_d = ε_s in absolute value, the split is exactly 50/50 — symmetric elasticities produce symmetric incidence. 2. The formula breaks down at ε_s = ε_d (which would require ε_s = ε_d with ε_s > 0 and ε_d < 0 — impossible in standard cases) — this singularity never occurs in practice. 3. The four extreme cases assume the respective elasticities are achieved globally; in reality, demand and supply become more elastic over longer time horizons, so incidence shifts as time passes — property owners bear more of a new land tax in the short run than the long run. 4. When a market has both a tax and an externality, the incidence formula still applies to the statutory tax, but welfare analysis must account for the external cost separately.
examTraps: A student might think perfectly elastic demand means buyers bear all the tax because elastic buyers respond strongly, but the correct answer is perfectly elastic demand means sellers bear all the tax — buyers simply leave the market at any higher price, so sellers must absorb the full tax to retain any customers at all.

A student might think perfectly elastic supply means sellers bear all the tax because 'elastic' suggests they are responsive to price, but the correct answer is perfectly elastic supply means buyers bear all the tax — sellers refuse to produce below their fixed minimum price, so buyers must pay the full tax as a higher Pb.

A student might think the government can choose tax incidence by deciding who legally pays, but the correct answer is economic incidence is entirely determined by elasticities — the government can change who writes the check but cannot change who actually bears the economic burden.

A student might think a 50/50 legal tax split (e.g., payroll tax) implies 50/50 economic incidence, but the correct answer is the economic split depends on ε_s/(ε_s - ε_d) — if |ε_d| is much lower than ε_s (inelastic labor supply), workers bear more than 50% even when the tax is legally split evenly.

frq.prompt: Consider four separate markets, each with a $4 unit tax: (A) perfectly inelastic demand, (B) perfectly elastic demand, (C) perfectly inelastic supply, (D) perfectly elastic supply.
parts: For each market, state whether buyers or sellers bear the full tax burden and briefly explain why using the elasticity formula.
For which of the four markets is there no deadweight loss? Explain the connection between elasticity, quantity change, and DWL.

A lobbyist argues that a tax on pharmaceutical companies falls entirely on companies, not patients. What would have to be true about the elasticities of supply and demand for this claim to be correct?
Explain why long-run incidence of a tax often differs from short-run incidence, using the concept that elasticities tend to rise over longer time horizons.

flashcards.front: Condition: perfectly inelastic demand — who bears the tax?
flashcards.back: Buyers bear 100% of the tax. Buyer share = ε_s/(ε_s - 0) = 1. Quantity is unchanged, Pb rises by t, Ps is unchanged, and DWL = 0. Buyers have no alternative but to pay the higher price.

flashcards.front: Condition: perfectly elastic demand — who bears the tax?
flashcards.back: Sellers bear 100% of the tax. Buyer share = ε_s/(ε_s - (-∞)) = 0. Ps falls by t, Pb is unchanged (stays at the demand-determined price), and quantity falls substantially, creating large DWL.

flashcards.front: Condition: perfectly inelastic supply — who bears the tax?
flashcards.back: Sellers bear 100% of the tax. Buyer share = 0/(0 - ε_d) = 0. Ps falls by t, Pb is unchanged, and quantity is fixed so DWL = 0. The classic example is a tax on fixed land — landowners absorb the full burden.

flashcards.front: Condition: perfectly elastic supply — who bears the tax?
flashcards.back: Buyers bear 100% of the tax. Buyer share = ∞/(∞ - ε_d) = 1. Pb rises by t, Ps is unchanged at the floor price, and quantity falls, generating DWL. Long-run competitive supply with constant costs approximates this case.

flashcards.front: Rule: relate elasticity comparison to incidence direction
flashcards.back: If |ε_d| < ε_s (demand more inelastic than supply), buyers bear the majority. If |ε_d| > ε_s (supply more inelastic than demand), sellers bear the majority. Equal absolute elasticities → 50/50 split.

flashcards.front: Distinction: short-run vs. long-run tax incidence
flashcards.back: Elasticities rise over longer time horizons as buyers and sellers find substitutes. A tax that falls mostly on buyers in the short run (inelastic short-run demand) will shift more toward sellers in the long run as buyers adapt. Incidence is not static.

flashcards.front: For which extreme elasticity cases is DWL = 0?
flashcards.back: DWL = 0 when either demand or supply is perfectly inelastic (vertical curve). In both cases, |ΔQ| = 0 — no trades are eliminated. The tax creates no efficiency loss, only a redistribution of surplus between buyers/sellers and the government.

flashcards.front: Buyer burden in dollars formula (general)
flashcards.back: Buyer burden = t · ε_s / (ε_s - ε_d). Seller burden = t · (-ε_d) / (ε_s - ε_d). These sum to t. A quick sanity check: if ε_d = -ε_s (equal absolute elasticities), each term = t/2, confirming a 50/50 split.

learn.openingTension: A firm wants to produce as much as possible, but nature imposes limits on what inputs can actually produce. How do economists formally describe the boundary between the feasible and the impossible — and why does the shape of that boundary matter for every decision a firm makes?
learn.coreIdea: Production technology is the complete set of feasible input-output combinations available to a firm. The production function f(L,K) captures the frontier of that set — the maximum output achievable from any given combination of labor and capital. Economists impose four standard assumptions on this set: free disposal (extra inputs never hurt), monotonicity (more inputs weakly raise output), and convexity of isoquants (smooth substitutability between inputs).
learn.mathSetup: Production set: Y = {(x,y) : y is feasible from inputs x}. Production function: Q = f(L,K) = max output achievable from (L,K). Key inequality: actual output ≤ f(L,K) for any feasible plan. Free disposal: if (L,K,Q) ∈ Y and Q' ≤ Q, then (L,K,Q') ∈ Y.
learn.derivation: 1. Define input vector x = (L,K) and output scalar Q. Economic reason: separating inputs from output lets us vary each independently. 2. Form the production set Y = {(x,Q) : Q achievable from x}. Economic reason: Y contains all technically feasible plans, including inefficient ones. 3. Extract the frontier: f(x) = max{Q : (x,Q) ∈ Y}. Economic reason: firms aiming to minimize cost will operate on the frontier, never inside it. 4. Impose free disposal: if (x,Q) ∈ Y then (x',Q') ∈ Y for all x' ≥ x, Q' ≤ Q. Economic reason: a firm can always throw away inputs or produce less — wasteful but never impossible. 5. Impose monotonicity: f is non-decreasing in each input. Economic reason: additional inputs cannot reduce maximum achievable output under free disposal. 6. Impose isoquant convexity: input sets {x : f(x) ≥ Q} are convex for all Q. Economic reason: balanced input mixes are at least as productive as extreme specialization, reflecting complementarity.
learn.graphicalIntuition: Plot capital K on the vertical axis and labor L on the horizontal axis. The production set is the region on or below any isoquant — but in input space, the feasible input set for a given output level is the region on or above the isoquant. Convexity means any weighted average of two input bundles that each produce Q also produces at least Q. On a graph, a convex isoquant bows inward toward the origin; a linear isoquant (perfect substitutes) and an L-shaped isoquant (perfect complements) are limiting cases. Common Misreading: students often confuse the production set (input-output space) with the isoquant map (input-input space) — the isoquant map is a projection of the production function's level sets, not the full production set.
learn.workedExample: Given: A bakery has production function f(L,K) = L^0.5 * K^0.5. Is the point (L=4, K=9, Q=7) in the production set? Find: Check feasibility. Solution: f(4,9) = 4^0.5 * 9^0.5 = 2 * 3 = 6. Since Q=7 > f(4,9)=6, the point (4,9,7) is NOT in the production set — it is technically infeasible. The point (4,9,5) IS in the set because 5 ≤ 6. Interpret: The production function defines the ceiling; any output at or below that ceiling is feasible via free disposal, but no plan can exceed it.
learn.boundaryConditions: Free disposal fails when inputs are bads (pollution, radioactive waste) — disposing of them is costly, so the production set no longer expands freely with extra inputs. Convexity of isoquants fails with increasing returns to specialization or non-convex technologies (e.g., assembly lines that only function at full capacity), meaning the efficient input set for a given output is non-convex and standard cost-minimization first-order conditions may yield only local optima.
examTraps: A student might think the production set Y contains only efficient input-output combinations, but the correct answer is that Y contains all feasible combinations including wasteful ones, because free disposal means a firm can always produce less than the maximum or use more inputs than necessary.

A student might think f(L,K) describes any output the firm could produce, but the correct answer is that f(L,K) describes only the maximum output achievable, because points below the frontier are feasible but points above it are not.

A student might think convexity of isoquants means the production function itself is concave, but the correct answer is that isoquant convexity (input sets are convex) is a weaker and different condition from concavity of f, because a function can have convex level sets without being globally concave.

A student might think monotonicity requires that more inputs always strictly increase output, but the correct answer is that monotonicity only requires weak increase (non-decreasing), because free disposal allows extra inputs to be ignored without harming production.

frq.prompt: A textile firm has production function f(L,K) = min(2L, K). It currently operates at L=3, K=4.
parts: Calculate the maximum output the firm can produce at (L=3, K=4).
Is the point (L=3, K=4, Q=7) in the production set Y? Justify using the definition of Y.
Does this production function satisfy free disposal? Show why or why not using a specific numerical example.
Explain in words why the isoquants for this production function are L-shaped rather than smoothly convex, and what that shape implies about input substitutability.

flashcards.front: What is the production set Y?
flashcards.back: Y = {(x,Q) : Q is feasible from inputs x} — the complete set of technically achievable input-output combinations, including inefficient ones. Every cost-minimizing firm operates on the frontier of Y, where Q = f(x).

flashcards.front: What does the production function f(L,K) represent?
flashcards.back: f(L,K) = the maximum output achievable from inputs (L,K). It is the upper frontier of the production set — actual output can be at or below f(L,K), but never above it. This ceiling property is what makes f useful for optimization.

flashcards.front: What is free disposal in production theory?
flashcards.back: Free disposal: if (x,Q) ∈ Y, then (x',Q') ∈ Y for all x' ≥ x and Q' ≤ Q. A firm can always use more inputs or produce less output without violating feasibility. Free disposal fails when inputs are costly to discard, such as hazardous waste.

flashcards.front: What does convexity of isoquants imply economically?
flashcards.back: Convex isoquants mean the input requirement set {x : f(x) ≥ Q} is convex — balanced input mixes are at least as productive as extreme ones. This reflects diminishing marginal returns to substitution and is violated by technologies with strong indivisibilities.

flashcards.front: How do you verify a point is in the production set?
flashcards.back: A point (L, K, Q) is in Y if and only if Q ≤ f(L, K). Compute the maximum output from (L,K); if stated output does not exceed it, the point is feasible. This directly applies the definition of Y as all output levels at or below the frontier.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between the production set and the isoquant map?
flashcards.back: The production set Y lives in input-output space (L, K, Q); the isoquant map lives in input space (L, K) and shows level curves Q = f(L,K) = Q̄. The isoquant map is a projection of the production function's level sets, not the full production set. Confusing the two leads to errors in identifying feasibility.

learn.openingTension: A restaurant can hire another cook in an hour but cannot expand its kitchen until the lease expires. Why does the same input — capital — behave so differently depending on the planning horizon, and how does this distinction reshape every cost and output decision the firm faces?
learn.coreIdea: Inputs are the factors of production — labor L (price w), capital K (price r), and land — combined to produce output Q = f(L,K). The crucial operational distinction is between fixed inputs (unchangeable in the short run, like a lease on equipment) and variable inputs (adjustable immediately, like hours of labor). The short run is defined as the period in which at least one input is fixed; the long run is the period in which all inputs are variable.
learn.mathSetup: Short-run production function: Q = f(L, K̄) where K̄ is the fixed capital stock. Variable input: L (can be chosen freely). Long-run production function: Q = f(L,K) where both L and K are choice variables. Input prices: wage w for labor, rental rate r for capital. Short-run cost: C = wL + rK̄ (only wL varies with output).
learn.derivation: 1. Define the input set: {L, K, Land, ...} with associated prices {w, r, ...}. Economic reason: each input has an opportunity cost that must be included in cost calculations. 2. Classify inputs by time horizon: fixed (K = K̄ in short run) vs. variable (L chosen optimally). Economic reason: contractual and physical constraints prevent immediate adjustment of some inputs. 3. Write short-run output: Q = f(L, K̄). Economic reason: with K fixed, only L can be varied to change Q, creating a single-input production problem in the short run. 4. Derive short-run cost: C(Q) = wL*(Q,K̄) + rK̄ where L*(Q,K̄) solves f(L,K̄) = Q. Economic reason: rK̄ is sunk in the short run — the firm pays it regardless of output choice. 5. In the long run, set K free: minimize wL + rK subject to f(L,K) = Q. Economic reason: all costs become variable when all inputs are adjustable, so long-run cost is always ≤ short-run cost for the same output.
learn.graphicalIntuition: In the short run, plot Q against L alone (holding K = K̄): this gives a one-dimensional production curve that rises at a decreasing rate (diminishing MP_L). In the long run, expand to the full (L,K) isoquant map: the firm can move along the isoquant to find the cost-minimizing input bundle. On a graph, the short-run constraint forces the firm to a single point on a given isoquant (wherever K = K̄ intersects it), while the long-run optimum is the tangency between the isoquant and the lowest isocost line. Common Misreading: students often treat 'short run' as a fixed calendar duration (e.g., one year), but the correct definition is purely about which inputs are fixed — for some industries (electric utilities), even five years is a short run for certain capital.
learn.workedExample: Given: A firm has f(L,K) = L^0.5 * K̄^0.5 with K̄ = 16, w = 10, r = 5. Find: (a) How much labor to produce Q=8 in the short run? (b) Short-run cost? Solution: (a) Set 8 = L^0.5 * 16^0.5 = L^0.5 * 4, so L^0.5 = 2, L = 4. (b) C = w*L + r*K̄ = 10*4 + 5*16 = 40 + 80 = 120. The fixed cost (80) is paid regardless of output. Interpret: In the long run, the firm could re-optimize K to reduce total cost; 120 is an upper bound on long-run cost for Q=8.
learn.boundaryConditions: The fixed/variable distinction breaks down for quasi-fixed inputs — inputs with a fixed cost to acquire but zero marginal cost once acquired (e.g., a software license): these create non-convexities in cost. The long run assumption that all inputs are variable ignores legal constraints (patents, regulations) and relationship-specific capital that cannot be redeployed, so 'long run' is an idealization that may never be fully reached.
examTraps: A student might think the short run always means less than one year, but the correct answer is that the short run is defined by the presence of at least one fixed input regardless of calendar time, because 'short' and 'long' are technological and contractual concepts, not temporal ones.

A student might think fixed costs are irrelevant to all decisions, but the correct answer is that fixed costs are irrelevant to short-run output decisions (they are sunk) but are relevant to long-run entry and exit decisions, because a firm exits in the long run if it cannot cover all costs including fixed ones.

A student might think capital is always the fixed input and labor is always the variable input, but the correct answer is that the classification depends on the context — skilled labor under long-term contracts can be fixed while leased equipment may be variable, because fixity is determined by contractual and adjustment constraints, not by the type of input.

A student might think the long-run cost is always higher than the short-run cost because the firm has more to pay, but the correct answer is that long-run cost is always less than or equal to short-run cost at the same output, because the long run offers strictly more flexibility and the firm can always replicate the short-run choice.

frq.prompt: A bakery has production function Q = 2L^0.5 * K^0.5. In the short run, K is fixed at K̄ = 9. The wage is w = 12 and the rental rate is r = 3.
parts: Derive the short-run labor demand function L*(Q, K̄=9) by solving Q = 2L^0.5 * 9^0.5 for L.
Write the short-run total cost function C(Q) = wL*(Q) + rK̄. Identify which component is fixed cost and which is variable cost.
If the bakery produces Q=12, calculate short-run total cost, fixed cost, and variable cost.
Explain in words why the firm's long-run cost for Q=12 cannot exceed the short-run cost you calculated, and under what condition they would be equal.

flashcards.front: What distinguishes a fixed input from a variable input?
flashcards.back: A fixed input cannot be changed within the current planning horizon (e.g., capital under a lease); a variable input can be freely adjusted (e.g., hourly labor). The short run has at least one fixed input; the long run has none. This distinction determines which costs are sunk and which are avoidable.

flashcards.front: What is the short-run production function?
flashcards.back: Q = f(L, K̄), where K̄ is the fixed capital stock. Only labor L is a choice variable. This reduces the firm's problem to a single dimension: how much labor to hire given K̄. The resulting cost function has both a fixed component (rK̄) and a variable component (wL*(Q)).

flashcards.front: Why is long-run cost always ≤ short-run cost for the same output?
flashcards.back: In the long run, all inputs are variable, so the firm can always choose the short-run input bundle as one feasible option. The long-run optimum is at least as good as any feasible point, so long-run cost ≤ short-run cost. They are equal only when the short-run capital K̄ happens to equal the long-run optimal K*.

flashcards.front: What are the input prices for labor and capital in the standard model?
flashcards.back: Labor price: wage w (dollars per unit of labor per period). Capital price: rental rate r (dollars per unit of capital per period). Whether the firm owns or leases capital, r represents the opportunity cost of using one unit of capital. These prices appear in the isocost line: wL + rK = C.

flashcards.front: What is a quasi-fixed input?
flashcards.back: A quasi-fixed input has a fixed acquisition cost but zero (or low) marginal cost once obtained, such as a software license or a factory setup cost. It creates non-convexities in cost: the firm either pays the fixed cost and uses the input freely, or does not acquire it at all. Quasi-fixed inputs complicate the clean fixed/variable dichotomy.

flashcards.front: How does fixing K at K̄ constrain the firm in input space?
flashcards.back: Fixing K = K̄ forces the firm to operate on the horizontal line K = K̄ in (L,K) space. The firm cannot move off this line in the short run, even if a different K would lower costs for a given isoquant. In the long run, the firm escapes this constraint and can reach the cost-minimizing point on any isoquant.

learn.openingTension: A consumer's indifference curve shows all baskets that give the same utility — but can the same geometric idea apply to a firm choosing inputs? It turns out the analogy is not just a metaphor: isoquants and indifference curves are mathematically identical objects, just interpreted in different economic contexts. Understanding this parallel is the fastest path to mastering both.
learn.coreIdea: An isoquant is the set of all input combinations (L,K) that produce exactly the same output level Q̄, defined by f(L,K) = Q̄.
